caption:
Comment ‘Watch’ and I’ll personally DM you the full conversation.

I spoke about women’s health for over 7 hours with four leading experts on the topic - this was our longest record ever!

There was so much value in our conversation that we decided to split it into two parts.

Today’s episode is part 2 of the conversation.

My hope with these episodes is to help remove stigma and give people language to understand what’s been misunderstood for too long. 

Thousands of you messaged to say how profoundly the first conversation impacted you.

This time we’re discussing how training, nutrition, and recovery should actually work for women.

@drstacysims is an exercise physiologist and nutrition scientist.

@nataliecrawfordmd is a fertility doctor helping women learn about their cycles and hormones.

@drmaryclaire is a menopause expert teaching women how to balance hormones and reduce inflammation.

@drvondawright is an orthopaedic surgeon and longevity researcher.

For decades, medicine and fitness have been built on male data.

Meaning women have been left confused and unsure about their bodies. That’s why this conversation needed to happen.

After Part 1, it was clear there was a lot more for us to uncover…

This time, we cover:

- Should women train differently across their cycle?
- What really happens during perimenopause and menopause?
- Why can lifting heavy can protect your brain and bones?
- The best type of exercise for female longevity…
- How to protect your hormones and energy as you age.

What I took away from this is how much most of us, especially men, don’t know about women’s health.

These episodes have made me realise how complex and powerful women’s bodies are.

I’m thankful to these doctors for helping make sense of something that countless people struggle to understand.

Even though I’ll never experience it myself, it’s a reminder that understanding this better helps everyone.

If you care about your health or the health of the women in your life, I believe this conversation will change the way you think.

taggedUsers.full_name: Mary Claire Haver, MD, author The New Menopause

caption:
I’m honored to share that after a 7-figure strategic investment from @steven and Steven.com, @hotsmartrich is officially joining @flightstory @flightstorystudio !!!
So where do I even begin to do justice to this moment?

How do I look back at the 22-year-old version of me who was told by her first boss out of college that she would never be successful unless she changed her voice and the way she looked, and tell her that everything she dreamed of would not only work out, but exceed her imagination?

This is not just the story of the last year building this show. It is the story of my life.

And it is the story and shared experience of so many women reading this.

Being underestimated. Being put in a box. Being discounted for things outside your control.

HSR was born the moment I decided I was done letting other people narrate my potential and I would take my power back with the exact thing my boss told me to change, my voice. 

I started the @hotsmartrich podcast with nothing but a mission, three iPhones, two portable mics, and my sister, despite the odds stacked against women in media, including the fact that only 7 percent of business podcasts are hosted by women (still true today!!). 

I wanted to create a sense of freedom for women everywhere who were tired of being judged, written off, or told to shrink. A space where ambition was not something to hide, but something to celebrate.

And then the HSR Angels showed up. You turned this into a movement. You turned this into a business, an investable business. You made this bigger than anything I could have imagined.

Which is why joining forces with @steven and @flightstory feels surreal. They are truly best in class, builders of culture, architects of global storytelling, and partners who believe as deeply as I do in what HSR can become.

I am grateful. I am in awe. I am inspired. But more than anything, I am motivated. The hard work starts now. 

Thank you to Ian Shepherd and @forbes for sharing the HSR story!

Images by @btswithwill 

(Continued in the comments)

caption:
Comment ‘Kamala’ and I’ll personally DM you the full conversation.

Kamala Harris made history as America’s first female, first Black, and first South Asian Vice President.

Today, she sits down with me for the longest interview she has ever had…

Before becoming Vice President, she served as California’s Attorney General and later as a U.S. Senator.

In a time when America feels divided and uncertain, Kamala told me I could ask her anything… and that’s what I did. 

We spoke candidly about the night she thought she’d won, a moment she says felt almost unreal comparing it to the loss of her mother.

We cover:

- What really happened the night she lost to Trump…
- Her complicated relationship with Joe Biden.
- When did she realise the White House was suppressing her?
- The phone call that made her furious before the debate.
- If she regrets not doing the Joe Rogan interview?
- What was it like pretending Biden was okay when she knew he wasn’t?

And… will she run for president again in 2028?

I didn’t want another interview full of talking points. I wanted to understand why Kamala cares so deeply about what she does and where that conviction comes from.

There were moments in our conversation where you could see how heavy it still is for her.

Her work has always been about people, shaped by empathy, service, and faith in what America stands for.

Regardless of where you stand politically, this conversation is about leadership, honesty, and what it takes to keep fighting when you’ve lost everything.

And as a podcaster, having these conversations are important as they shed light on the inner workings of the people and systems that govern us.

Watch the full episode now on YouTube: search “The Diary Of A CEO Kamala Harris.

musicInfo.song_name: The Diary Of A CEO x Kamala Harris

Grapevines are incredibly strong and resilient, with mature tendrils capable of supporting 50 to 100 pounds and woody vines tough enough to hold up heavy tree limbs. These woody perennials can live for over 40 years, requiring robust, permanent trellising to support their significant weight and rapid, vigorous growth. 
Key Strength Characteristics:
Structural Strength: Mature vines are wiry and dense, capable of holding up hundreds of pounds of fruit and foliage, often requiring sturdy steel supports.
Resilience: They are highly durable, able to survive harsh winters and even bounce back after being cut to the ground.
Weight Capacity: A single, mature grapevine can produce up to 20 or more pounds of fruit annually.

John 15:5
“I am the vine; you are the branches. If you remain in me and I in you, you will bear much fruit; apart from me you can do nothing.

caption:
She said Yes 💍🥺

To say I was nervous is an understatement… i could feel my heart racing in my chest. She thought I was away in Europe - but I had snuck into Morocco on Christmas Day where she was on holiday with her family - I asked her mother and father if I could ask her to marry me - then had to build this big hut outside because a thunderstorm was forecast last minute 😅 - and then I stepped out and surprised her when she thought she was meeting her fam for dinner. Thankfully she said yes. 

10 years ago, I didn’t really understand the concept of marriage as many of you know - I was definitely an “avoidant” - but when you meet the right person, a lot of things start to make a lot of sense ❤️

Update: thank you for all the kind words and congratulations!!!

caption:
Comment ‘Tony’ and I’ll personally DM you the full conversation.

Most people think change comes from motivation…

Today’s guest has helped over 50 million people overcome their limiting beliefs, and he believes it comes from understanding the forces shaping your decisions when no one is watching.

@tonyrobbins, welcome to The Diary Of A CEO. 

When I first started talking to Tony, what surprised me most wasn’t his success - it was where he came from.

Before the businesses, before the influence, before the public image people recognise today, Tony grew up in a household full of violence and instability, and an abusive mother. 

And as the conversation began, I found myself wanting to understand how someone can come from that kind of starting point and still build a life with so much direction and steadiness.

That curiosity shaped this conversation. Not how to be successful, but how people become who they are when their early circumstances don’t appear to set them up.

Tony has always been driven by a desire to change how people feel in the moment, from making cookies at Easter just to see people light up, to spending decades working with people at every level, from individuals trying to get unstuck to leaders operating under intense pressure.

In this conversation, I asked:
* How do people rebuild trust in themselves after failure?
* What happens when success outpaces emotional maturity?
* What actually drives our decisions under pressure?
* How do you take responsibility without burning out?
* How do early emotional patterns shape adult behaviour?

There’s a moment in this conversation where I bring out a letter from Marc Benioff, founder of Salesforce, and read it to Tony.

As I read it, the emotions from the letter caught him off guard - it’s one of those moments where Tony saw the impact of his work and how much he changed Marc’s life.

You don’t spend decades doing this kind of work, reaching people at that depth, unless there’s something real behind it. 

I’m looking forward to hearing your thoughts on this one, let me know them after you’re finished watching.

caption:
We recently hit 13M subscribers on YouTube, but how did we actually get here?

2017 ——> 2025

It took us 4 years before we saw meaningful rapid growth. 

Fast forward 8 years, here’s my advice for every creator starting out today:

Out persist yourself. You need to love what you do.  Once you’re an adult you only get 6 or 7 decades so make sure whatever your mission is it’s worth your time.

Your team will sway your outcome the most. The best decisions you make will be people. The worst decisions you make will be people.

Don’t take it all so seriously. This sounds weird, but one of the most clarifying thoughts that’s helped me deal with the feedback is realising that I’m going to die someday, and that day is relatively soon. You’re going to be nothing more than a memory in a few loved ones minds.

That will liberate you from worry, thinking too much about others and enables you to do YOU with all of your heart.

Thank you for enabling me & 100+ of my team here in the UK to have a job we love just by tuning in and supporting us ❤️

This is a celebration for this community hitting 13M subscribers x 

Who do you want to see on DOAC next?

The line about people decisions is the truest thing here. Best and worst decisions across all 12 of my businesses have been people. Not strategy. Not product. People. Took me years and nearly losing a business to learn that hiring for character and building systems around skill gaps beats hiring for talent and hoping the culture sorts itself out. Persistence gets you in the game. People decisions determine if you stay.

Watched this one twice. The point about persistence and people decisions hits hard. We've invested in companies through GEE Capital where the founder's biggest asset wasn't the business model. It was the fact they kept showing up when nobody was watching. Four years of no traction sounds brutal. But that's exactly when the real compound effect starts. Most people quit at year two.

caption:
Comment ‘Watch’ and I’ll personally DM you the full conversation.

Why is women’s health still so misunderstood?

To answer that, today I’m joined by four world-leading experts redefining women’s health.

@drstacysims is an exercise physiologist and nutrition scientist transforming female performance.

@nataliecrawfordmd is a fertility doctor helping women understand their bodies and cycles.

@drmaryclaire is a menopause expert and creator of The Galveston Diet for hormone balance.

@drvondawright is an orthopaedic surgeon and longevity researcher guiding women stay strong for life.

Women are the majority of the population, yet less than 2% of medical research focuses on female-specific conditions. 

For decades, medicine was built on male data and it’s left women misdiagnosed, misunderstood, and underrepresented.

When women don’t get the right care, we all lose.

We cover:

- What’s really happening to women’s hormones at different life stages?
- Why do so many women feel unheard by their doctors?
- Is birth control doing more harm than good?
- What doctors still get wrong about menopause and fertility?
- Why understanding your cycle could change your health forever…

What stood out to me most was how much we still don’t know about women’s health. 

Hearing how often women are dismissed or misdiagnosed was shocking.

You might think this episode’s only for women, but the truth is it’s for all of us.

As a man, I’ll never experience it myself, but conversations like this have made me realise how important it is that we all understand the female body better.

This is Part 1 of our discussion, and Part 2 is coming next where we go deeper into the emotional, social, and relational side of women’s health.

taggedUsers.full_name: Mary Claire Haver, MD, author The New Menopause

musicInfo.song_name: The Diary Of A CEO x Women’s Health Debate

caption:
Comment “Michelle” and I’ll personally DM you our full conversation. 

I’m honoured to welcome the former First Lady of the United States @michelleobama and her older brother @craigmalrob to DOAC.

I met Michelle a few weeks ago and instantly felt like I’d known her for a decade - we talked about everything, love, education, Barack, her children, university, Al, childhood, health, yoga, and then I did her podcast that day.

The day after she came to my studio to record her DOAC episode and I loved it - I’ve really enjoyed getting to know her - the grace - the class - the wisdom - the humour.

On and off camera, an absolute warm, kind, class act by the way she conducts herself, you’d never guess that she has lived the life that she has lived.

It was also illuminating. What began as a conversation about leadership became something deeper - an honest and intimate exploration of her story.

We cover:
- The reason why she didn’t attend Trump’s inauguration…
- Addressing the rumours swirling around her & Barack’s marriage.
- What she wished she had asked for from Barack before entering The White House?
- Navigating life in the White House, grief, personal boundaries, and rediscovering her true calling.
- Craig & Michelle opening up about the importance of having unconditional love at home.
- Fertility struggles, parenting tips, and what it really takes to sustain a long-term marriage.
and so much more!

This conversation redefines what it means to belong, to lead, and to slow down with intention.

I, for one, have never heard her share her story like this before - I’m happy it’s finally out in the world.

And importantly, a massive thank you to my team for helping make this happen x

Watch the full episode now on YouTube: Search “The Diary of a CEO Michelle Obama”

taggedUsers.full_name: IMO with Michelle Obama & Craig Robinson

No website. No coding. No guesswork!

I’ve seen first-hand how powerful this is. And I’m not just backing it with my words — I’ve become a co-owner to help more people take control of their income!

caption:
Comment ‘Louis’ and I’ll personally DM you the full conversation.

He’s sold over 100 million records, performed in front of millions, and been part of one of the most famous bands in history.

Almost no one has achieved that.

But today’s guest Louis Tomlinson has.

At just 18, Simon Cowell chose @louist91 to be part of a new boy band called One Direction.

Overnight, he was selling out stadiums all around the globe and breaking records.

But then what followed was the band falling apart, having to build a solo career from scratch, and the unimaginable loss of his sister, Mum and bandmate Liam Payne. 

I haven’t seen Louis be this open and honest before - he spoke about how losing different loved ones leads to completely different kinds of pain.

We cover:

- What happens when fame strips away your sense of self?
- How do you rebuild after losing the people you love most?
- How did the loss of Liam Payne affect Louis?
- How grief shaped his music and his view of the world…

What really stood out to me about Louis is how grounded he is. After everything he’s achieved, the fame & the success, he still talks about his fans and his family like they’re his anchor.

You can see how much he’s grown not just as an artist but as a person.

Hearing him speak about building things on his own, from his festival to his brand and his new music, you realise he’s finally getting to define who Louis Tomlinson really is on his own terms.

I’m really looking forward to hearing your thoughts on this one, let me know them after you’re finished watching.

caption:
Yesterday, it would have been Princess Diana’s 64th birthday. Prince William spent the day in Sheffield with myself and Gordon Brown fighting to end homelessness – a cause deeply close to his late mother’s heart.

We were in Sheffield to mark the second anniversary of Homewards, a five-year programme led by @princeandprincessofwales.

Its mission: to show that homelessness can be ended – not just managed.

Prince William and I first travelled to Bournemouth to spend time with local leaders.

We then travelled to Aberdeen to bring together the business community and meet charity leaders and young people at risk of homelessness.

And yesterday, we travelled to Sheffield – bringing together local leaders, frontline workers, business leaders, charities and young people with lived experience to reflect on the progress so far… and, more importantly, to ask: how do we scale this?

My role has been to serve as an ambassador for the project – representing the business community and exploring how private sector thinking, talent and investment can support systemic social change.

People often ask me: What is Prince William like? What’s he like to work with?

Over the last two years, I’ve spent time with him in both public and private settings – and I have to say, I have the utmost respect for him.

He carries an incredible weight of responsibility and does it with class, humility, patience and humanity.

I see how he interacts with everyone – how deeply he listens and how gracious he is.

And he works so hard. I remarked recently that he works like he’s running out of time.

He truly reminds me of everything people say about Princess Diana – a big heart, big ambitions, and a remarkable way with people.

I’m honoured to help him in any way I can to achieve some of those big goals.

On a personal note, my Nigerian mother and British father were lifelong fans of both Diana and the royal family – so they’re in proud disbelief that I’ve been able to play a small part in this mission!

But… the hard work is only just beginning!

caption:
this will never not feel weird...😵‍💫 today I found out I’m in TIME magazine in their “TIME100 top influential voices” list... I have to say...

If there’s one thing I’ve learned from all this, it’s that embarrassment is the price of entry - and most people just aren’t willing to pay it. 

they never hit record, write the post, or launch the thing… because the thought of “Dave from work” seeing it and making a snide comment is too much. 

that’s so tragic to me.

how much potential are we wasting because we’re scared of what Jenny from our hometown, our mum, or - worst of all - our supposed “friends” might think? 

so many people hold their potential back because they’re scared of what some half-friend on Facebook might think - someone they haven’t properly spoken to since Year 11

I don’t think anything holds us back from the life we could have lived more than the opinions of people who don’t even know us.

pressing record that first time felt cringey, awkward, and exposing - but discomfort has so often been a doorway, so I’m ok with it now. I’m okay with people thinking what they wanna think and saying what they wanna say. 

at the end of the day, I’m going to die someday - and so are you. 

So you might as well live.

too many people wait until they feel ready, polished, or ‘credible’ enough to start but every good thing that’s happened in my life started with me accepting that I would have to inevitably “practice in public” - be bad at something in front of the world before I’d figured it out

are you willing to practice in public? 

anyway...big love to my team at FLIGHTSTORY // The Diary Of A CEO - they’re the very best in the industry - they deserve all the credit and work relentless to fight for every 1% gain we can find

in my head, this kind of recognition is usually reserved for big US Hollywood stars… not us, from a bedroom in Manchester, England

an Immense thanks to every guest who’s shared their story on my show and to everyone who’s ever tuned in on a commute, dog walk or at night time - absolutely loving chasing curiosity with you and your feedback and support has been a huge motivator through it all xxx

Image owned by: @catherineharbour

caption:
Why did we stay independent??? Today we’re the daily cover of Forbes which is so insane to me 😵‍💫😮❤️ Forbes came to our HQ and did an all-access documentary for their YouTube channel about what we’re up to at @flightstory - they followed us for a few days to make the most in depth, detailed piece I I’ve ever seen on our media company and investment fund. You can view it right now on the Forbes YouTube channel. Most certainly the most revealing piece I’ve ever seen about our company. 

I wanted to respond to the headline and give some honest clarity on why we didn’t take an undisclosed deal we were offered last year - that many of my podcasting counterparts have taken. First and foremost let me preface this by saying - there is no right or wrong answer here, every podcaster is different and is in a different situation. We did what we felt was right for us. The deal offered meant: 

1) It would have meant 300% more ads in DOAC (minimum) - I know y’all want less ads in podcasts, not more. 

2) It would have meant we had less controls / different incentives relating to which platforms we focus on.

3) These big numbers you see podcasters getting aren’t what they seem. You’re not transferred x million dollars into your bank account, the money is guaranteed over X years if you sign the deal - but with that comes a lot more obligations that aren’t always in the best interests of the show. 

4) When our Chief revenue officer @christiana_brenton ran the numbers with her team, she believed we could do better on our own, over the coming years, via our own media company @flightstory. 

5) We have almost 100 people full time working on our media company - led by @georgievholt. They’re the best in the world imo. We believed we have more talent and skill internally than an external partner could offer us, so this is a bet on our team and our talent. 

Time will tell! 

Hopefully that gives clarity as to why we’ve chosen to remain independent and build our own media company ❤️🚀👊🏾

Reporting by @alexy0rk

caption:
I’ve been thinking about gratitude… do you agree? MY TEST RESULTS 👇🏾

Today, I did a DEXA scan (which shows exactly how much muscle, fat, and bone mass I have - one of the strongest predictors of longevity) and a VO2 max test (which measures how efficiently my body uses oxygen - one of the strongest predictors of long-term health).

A low VO2 max increases your risk of death more than smoking, diabetes, and high blood pressure combined. And after 30, we lose about 3-8% of muscle per decade if we don’t fight to keep it.

My results: 

1. My vo2 max was in the “high performance range”. Frankly I want to improve this. Peter’s team wants to move it up 5 points~ to the elite range. So going to run longer distances. Elite range is 55ish~

2. They said I have “zero” visceral fat which they said was an unusual finding (the fat around organs - not to be confused with body fat). Results said: You have zero visceral adipose tissue, an uncommon but excellent finding (for the metabolically harmful fat which sits around your organs).

3. My bone density was very high (probably why I can’t float in the water). Results said: In plain terms, your bones look strong and healthy. If you want to keep it that way, continue weight-bearing exercise, ensure adequate calcium and vitamin D intake, and maintain a generally active lifestyle.

4. Muscle results said: You are very well-muscled (in the top 10% of population for age/gender in ALMI and FFMI), a great platform for longevity and strength. 

5. My body fat % was considered normal, which is higher than I’d like - going to work on that - now back on keto!

latestComments.text: Everyone should understand and know their VO2MAX 🔥 #VO2ToTheMax
@steven did you manage sub 20 5km? That’s my goal 2026
Genuinely… always find value in your 1 on 1 pep talk style videos. I wish people like you had stats on the profound impact you provide to millions of souls a month
Trev also need to do this

Gratitude is the best attitude it’s why I have lost everything x3 times and still get up and thankful, grateful for everything not what I don’t have!! ❤️ the western world is so caught up in consumerism and materialism that sometimes taking everything away you are left with nature, a house and sometimes it is the simple things that brings it all back to the present moment to be truly grateful for!!! ❤️ Love this ❤️

caption:
Comment ‘Matthew’ and I’ll personally DM you the full conversation.

Today’s guest has one of the most distinctive voices in the world.

And for the first time, you’ll get to hear a completely different side of him.

It’s an honour to welcome Matthew McConaughey to The Diary Of A CEO.

Over the last thirty years @officiallymcconaughey has shown the world what it means to be a leading man in Hollywood, but what’s most interesting is how he’s managed to reinvent himself in ways few people ever believed were possible.

His story goes way beyond his roles in Interstellar, Dallas Buyers Club, or The Wolf of Wall Street.

It’s the way he’s written his own story both on and off the screen.

From turning down $14.5 million at the height of his rom-com career to wrestling with what faith, masculinity, and purpose really mean, Matthew has never taken the easy path…

So in this conversation we cover:

- Why comfort might be the biggest disease of our time.
- The decision that changed Matthew’s life forever…
- The dangerous trap of success?
- What is the silent crisis young men are facing today?
- How faith, resistance, and struggle give life its meaning?

and so much more…

What I appreciate most about Matthew is that he’s a role model. Not just for me but for so many young men and really for anyone who listens to him. 

His book Poems and Prayers is proof of that. When I read it, it took me somewhere else, somewhere I needed to go. And I think it will do the same for a lot of people.

I’m looking forward to hearing your thoughts on this one, let me know them after you’re finished watching.

Watch the full episode now on YouTube: search “The Diary of a CEO Matthew McConaughey”

latestComments.text: 2 absolutely great humans of our time…

musicInfo.song_name: The Diary Of A CEO x Matthew McConaughey

caption:
Are you sabotaging yourself by taking things too personally?

Comment ‘Agent’ and I’ll personally DM you our latest conversation! 

Almost seven million of you were blown away by my conversation with @evypoumpouras last year for her insane talent for spotting lies, so we had to bring her back for more. 

Evy is a U.S Secret Service agent turned behaviour expert that has faced terrorists, protected presidents, and today she’s back on The Diary Of A CEO for her most unfiltered interview yet.

This time, she’s tackling so many unanswered questions - including the one we’ve never instinctively asked ourselves: How do you walk into any room and command respect without saying anything?

She teaches people how to think like a Secret Service agent, how to stay calm under pressure, and how to handle fear like a pro.

In this episode, we cover:
• Why getting offended is actually leaving you powerless?
• The 4 words you can say to guarantee success in any situation!
• How to master fear and stop it from controlling your life!
• Why you should never listen to anyone and trust your gut!
And so much more!

Evy has an incredible talent for storytelling and giving genuine practical advice that will be life changing for so many of you, especially in overcoming and breaking free from fear.

When she talks about resilience, and commanding respect, and overcoming manipulation, she isn’t quoting from a self-help book—she’s lived it.

Let me know your thoughts below after watching!

Watch the full episode now on YouTube—search ‘The Diary of a CEO Evy’

caption:
A wonderful trip up to Scotland with Prince William to meet business owners and continue our work to end homelessness 👇🏾

📞 More than a year ago Prince William’s team asked me to become an ambassador for @homewardsuk, his initiative built to prove that homelessness can be ended, not just managed, by creating long-term solutions, with multiple stakeholders, in local communities around the UK.

🙏🏾 My role has been to use my experience in business to help Homewards explore how employers can improve the odds for young people at risk of homelessness or from disadvantaged backgrounds.

🛬 Myself and Prince William first travelled to Bournemouth to meet business owners, young people and charities in the region and today up to Aberdeen to speak to business leaders, charities and young people that are at-risk of homelessness.

🧠 Prince William and Homewards realise that homelessness is an incredibly complex, systemic challenge. It’s not just a housing issue – as I learnt directly from the young people I spent time with today, it’s a mental health challenge, a skills challenge, a confidence challenge, a community challenge, a family breakdown challenge and so much more.

👑 Spending time with Prince William has been incredibly illuminating. He’s incredibly kind, patient, extremely hard working, deeply knowledgeable, and incredibly gifted at bringing people together.

❤️ He’s got a big heart, big ambitions, and a quiet determination to use his platform for good. What struck me most is how deeply he listens – not just to experts, but to those with lived experience on an issue. 

On a personal note, this whole experience has been incredibly surreal - so much of an honour that it’s hard to understand or articulate - I’m deeply grateful that Prince William would involve me in his work, and genuinely honoured that he takes the time to seek out my opinion on so many issues ❤️👊🏾

latestComments.text: Powerful work Steven! Love seeing business used for real change.
So great to see this issue being addressed in a meaningful way
Amazing to see you both dedicated to such an important cause! Ending homelessness is a huge undertaking, and it's inspiring to see this work being done.

caption:
During today’s run it dawned on me how sorely I’m going to miss my health, mobility and strength when it’s all gone.

Our health is a temporary loan.

There really is no greater expression of gratitude than taking care of it.

And the things we express gratitude for tend to stick around a little bit longer.

———

Audio taken from my conversation with @robertgreeneofficial on @thediaryofaceopodcast

📹: @btswithwill

Seeing. Walking. Typing. Tying your shoes.

Gratitude isn’t about having more,
it’s about noticing what you’d miss if it disappeared.

What’s one thing you’re thankful you can still do today?

caption:
Today was surreal! Prince William's team asked me to spend the day with him visiting businesses in the south of England that are doing great work to end homelessness and to partner with him and his Foundation @homewardsuk to work to end Homelessness in the next 5 years! This is part of a multi-year campaign that me and Prince William are embarking on to change the state of homelessness in the UK.

🥪 We started the day in a Pret A Manger in Bournemouth! We both put on aprons, made coffee's, sandwiches, and got to meet some of the incredible team members that are former refugees, former inmates and formerly homeless!

⚽️ We drove over to Bournemouth FC and met players from the Men's and Women's team who had come through the bounce back program and used football to change their lives. 

🎤 I was lucky enough to have a few moments to ask the future king some questions on his own personal motivations and how we manages his time! 

👩🏻‍💼 Myself and Prince William then hosted a business round table with a group of local business leaders about how they could work to hire more homeless individuals into their organisation! 

This is just the beginning of a big piece of work we're planning to do with Prince William, Homewards and the UK business community to help get homeless people (and former inmates) back into work, to up-skill them and to end homelessness for good!

🤯 Side note:

If you had told me as a kid, I would be spending the day with Prince William, the future King - I would never ever ever believed you. I grew up with a Nigerian mother and a english dad who both adore the royal family, one of my earliest memories is watching my mum and dad cry when Diana died. William has a wonderful way with people, and he was authentically kind both on and off camera today! Thank you to whatever force in the universe put me there today, I'm so grateful and a little confused. A day I'm sure I'll tell my kids about. 

If you're a business please do check out Homewards work! Together as a business community we can do a lot more to get all the talent we have in this country back to work! ❤️

latestComments.text: Wow, what an incredible experience! It’s inspiring to see such leadership and compassion making a real difference—congratulations on this amazing partnership!
Great to see Stephen and Team. 👏
